title: Jet Li

Huang Qiuyan  ​  ​ (m. 1987; div. 1990)​      [Nina Li Chi](/wiki/Nina_Li_Chi "Nina Li Chi")  ​ (m. 1999)​

([courtesy name](/wiki/Courtesy_name "Courtesy name") **Yangzhong**; born 26 April 1963)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [martial artist](/wiki/Martial_artist "Martial artist"), actor, and philanthropist. With [a career](/wiki/Jet_Li_filmography "Jet Li filmography") spanning more than forty years, he is regarded as one of the most iconic Chinese film stars and one of the greatest martial artists in the history of cinema.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) His film career in Asia is credited with reviving Hong Kong kungfu films[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) as well as [Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Monastery "Shaolin Monastery").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

**Jet Li Lianjie** ([courtesy name](/wiki/Courtesy_name "Courtesy name") **Yangzhong**; born 26 April 1963)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [martial artist](/wiki/Martial_artist "Martial artist"), actor, and philanthropist. With [a career](/wiki/Jet_Li_filmography "Jet Li filmography") spanning more than forty years, he is regarded as one of the most iconic Chinese film stars and one of the greatest martial artists in the history of cinema.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) His film career in Asia is credited with reviving Hong Kong kungfu films[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) as well as [Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Monastery "Shaolin Monastery").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Li was trained as a [wushu](/wiki/Wushu_(sport) "Wushu (sport)") athlete at the Beijing Shichahai Sports School and went on to win multiple national championships with the [Beijing Wushu Team](/wiki/Beijing_Wushu_Team "Beijing Wushu Team") between 1974 and 1979.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) After his retirement from the sport in 1979, he made his acting debut with the Hong Kong film *[Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Temple_(1982_film) "Shaolin Temple (1982 film)")* (1982),[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) a runaway success followed by two sequels in [1984](/wiki/Kids_From_Shaolin "Kids From Shaolin") and [1986](/wiki/Martial_Arts_of_Shaolin "Martial Arts of Shaolin"). Li established himself as a leading action star with the *[Once Upon a Time in China](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_China_(film_series) "Once Upon a Time in China (film series)")* series (1991–1993), in which he portrayed Chinese folk hero [Wong Fei-hung](/wiki/Wong_Fei-hung "Wong Fei-hung"), followed by *[Born to Defence](/wiki/Born_to_Defence "Born to Defence")* (1988), which is his directorial debut, *[Swordsman II](/wiki/Swordsman_II "Swordsman II")* (1992), *[Fong Sai-yuk](/wiki/Fong_Sai-yuk_(film) "Fong Sai-yuk (film)")* (1993), *[Fist of Legend](/wiki/Fist_of_Legend "Fist of Legend")* (1994), *[High Risk](/wiki/High_Risk_(1995_film) "High Risk (1995 film)")* (1995), *[Black Mask](/wiki/Black_Mask_(1996_film) "Black Mask (1996 film)")* (1996), and *[Hitman](/wiki/Hitman_(1998_film) "Hitman (1998 film)")* (1998).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Li made his Hollywood debut as a villain in *[Lethal Weapon 4](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_4 "Lethal Weapon 4")* (1998), followed by his first Hollywood leading role in *[Romeo Must Die](/wiki/Romeo_Must_Die "Romeo Must Die")* (2000). He went on to star in international productions such as [Luc Besson](/wiki/Luc_Besson "Luc Besson")-produced films *[Kiss of the Dragon](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Dragon "Kiss of the Dragon")* (2001) and *[Unleashed](/wiki/Unleashed_(2005_film) "Unleashed (2005 film)")* (2005). He returned to Asia with [*Hero*](/wiki/Hero_(2002_film) "Hero (2002 film)") (2002), followed by [*Fearless*](/wiki/Fearless_(2006_film) "Fearless (2006 film)") (2006) and *[The Warlords](/wiki/The_Warlords "The Warlords")* (2007). In 2008, he starred in *[The Mummy: Tomb of the Dragon Emperor](/wiki/The_Mummy:_Tomb_of_the_Dragon_Emperor "The Mummy: Tomb of the Dragon Emperor")* and *[The Forbidden Kingdom](/wiki/The_Forbidden_Kingdom "The Forbidden Kingdom")*, which marked his first collaboration with fellow kungfu star [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan"). From 2010 to 2014, he appeared in the first three of the action film series *[The Expendables](/wiki/The_Expendables_(franchise) "The Expendables (franchise)")*.

As a philanthropist, Li founded [One Foundation](/wiki/One_Foundation "One Foundation") in 2007, which was registered in 2011 as the first private charitable fundraising organization in China.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) He also co-founded *Taiji Zen*, an online health program providing instruction in [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation") and [tai chi](/wiki/Tai_chi "Tai chi").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Li topped *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*'s list of China's richest stars in 2004[[15]](#cite_note-15) and ranked 10th on the [*Forbes* China Celebrity 100](/wiki/Forbes_China_Celebrity_100 "Forbes China Celebrity 100") list in 2004, 3rd in 2008, 6th in 2009, 24th in 2010, 8th in 2011, 22nd in 2012, and 41st in 2013. He was named by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine as one of the [100 most influential people](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in 2010.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Early life and martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and martial arts career")]

Li was born in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), and was the youngest of two boys and two girls. His [ancestral home](/wiki/Ancestral_home_(Chinese) "Ancestral home (Chinese)") is in [Shenyang, Liaoning](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang"). When he was two years old, his father died and his family then lived in poverty.[[17]](#cite_note-personal-bio-1-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Li was eight when his talent for [wushu](/wiki/Wushu_(sport) "Wushu (sport)") was noticed as he practiced at a school summer course at the Beijing Sports and Exercise School (now known as [Beijing Sport University](/wiki/Beijing_Sport_University "Beijing Sport University")).[[19]](#cite_note-:10-19)[[20]](#cite_note-personal-bio-2-20) Renowned coaches [Li Junfeng](/wiki/Li_Junfeng "Li Junfeng") and [Wu Bin](/wiki/Wu_Bin_(Wushu_Coach) "Wu Bin (Wushu Coach)")[[21]](#cite_note-talktalk1-21) made extra efforts to help the talented boy develop. Wu Bin even bought food for Li's family in order to boost Li's protein intake.[[21]](#cite_note-talktalk1-21)

At the age of eleven, Li became a member of the [1974 China national wushu team](/wiki/1974_China_national_wushu_team "1974 China national wushu team") which traveled to the United States during the time of the [Ping-pong diplomacy](/wiki/Ping-pong_diplomacy "Ping-pong diplomacy").[[22]](#cite_note-:Minami-22) The group performed for American audiences in Hawaii, San Francisco, New York, and Washington, D.C., and additionally in Mexico City and Hong Kong.[[22]](#cite_note-:Minami-22) When the [Chinese National Wushu Team](/wiki/China_national_wushu_team "China national wushu team") went to perform for President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in the United States, he was asked by Nixon to be his personal bodyguard. Li replied, "I don't want to protect any individual. When I grow up, I want to defend my one billion Chinese countrymen!"[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In November of the same year, the [Beijing Wushu Team](/wiki/Beijing_Wushu_Team "Beijing Wushu Team") was founded and Li became a member.[[24]](#cite_note-personal-bio-9-24) He became the national all-around champion four times from 1975 to 1979.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

> My winning first place caused quite a sensation because I was so young. I was 12 years old, and the other two medalists were in their mid- to late twenties. During the awards ceremony, as I stood on the top step of the podium, I was still shorter than the 2nd and 3rd place medalists. It must have been quite a sight.

— Jet Li *China's Internet Celebrity*[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Li is a master of several styles of wushu, especially [changquan](/wiki/Changquan "Changquan") and [fanziquan](/wiki/Fanziquan "Fanziquan"). He has also studied other arts including [baguazhang](/wiki/Baguazhang "Baguazhang"), [taijiquan](/wiki/Tai_chi "Tai chi"), [xingyiquan](/wiki/Xingyiquan "Xingyiquan"), [zuiquan](/wiki/Drunken_boxing "Drunken boxing") (drunken fist), [yingzhaoquan](/wiki/Eagle_Claw "Eagle Claw") (eagle claw), and [tanglangquan](/wiki/Northern_Praying_Mantis_(martial_art) "Northern Praying Mantis (martial art)") (praying mantis fist). He also practiced weapons such as [gunshu](/wiki/Gun_(staff) "Gun (staff)"), [daoshu](/wiki/Dao_(Chinese_sword) "Dao (Chinese sword)"), and [jianshu](/wiki/Jian "Jian") as well as [sanjiegun](/wiki/Three-section_staff "Three-section staff") (three section staff).[[27]](#cite_note-wushuography-27)

Li retired from competitive wushu when he was only 18 due to a knee injury,[[28]](#cite_note-28) but became an assistant coach of the Beijing Wushu team for a few years. Li's martial arts prowess would eventually contribute to his domestic and international fame.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2023, Li was inducted into the [Martial Arts History Museum](/wiki/Martial_Arts_History_Museum "Martial Arts History Museum") Hall of Fame.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Acting career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Acting career")]

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Asia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/JetLiHK.jpg/250px-JetLiHK.jpg)](/wiki/File:JetLiHK.jpg)

Li's hand print and autograph at the [Avenue of Stars](/wiki/Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong "Avenue of Stars, Hong Kong") in Hong Kong

The fame gained by his sports winnings led to a career as a martial arts film star, beginning in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") and then continuing into [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). Li acquired his screen name in 1982 in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") when a publicity company thought his real name was too hard to pronounce. They likened his career to an aircraft, which likewise "takes-off" as quickly, so they placed the name Jet Li on the movie posters.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Soon everybody was calling him by this new name, which was also based on the nickname, "Jet", given to him as a young student, due to his speed and grace when training with the Beijing Wushu team.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

He made his film debut with the 1982 film *[Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Temple_(1982_film) "Shaolin Temple (1982 film)")*. The film broke [box office records in China](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_China "List of highest-grossing films in China"), grossing CN¥161,578,014 (US$85,376,199) at the Chinese box office,[[32]](#cite_note-32) from an estimated 500 million [ticket sales](/wiki/List_of_films_by_box_office_admissions "List of films by box office admissions").[[33]](#cite_note-:7-33)[[34]](#cite_note-:8-34)[[35]](#cite_note-:0-35) The sequel *[Kids From Shaolin](/wiki/Kids_From_Shaolin "Kids From Shaolin")* sold an estimated 490 million tickets, making it 1984's [highest-grossing film in China](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_China "List of highest-grossing films in China").[[36]](#cite_note-Yearbook-36)

Some of his more famous Chinese films include:

* The *[Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Temple_(1982_film) "Shaolin Temple (1982 film)")* series (1, 2 and 3), which are considered to be the films which sparked the rebirth of the real [Shaolin Temple](/wiki/Shaolin_Monastery "Shaolin Monastery") in Dengfeng, China;
* The *[Once Upon a Time in China](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_China "Once Upon a Time in China")* series (Chinese title: *Wong Fei Hung*), about the legendary Chinese folk hero Master [Wong Fei Hung](/wiki/Wong_Fei_Hung "Wong Fei Hung").
* *[Fist of Legend](/wiki/Fist_of_Legend "Fist of Legend")* (Chinese title: *Jing Wu Ying Xiong*), a remake of [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee")'s *[Fist of Fury](/wiki/Fist_of_Fury "Fist of Fury")* (1972).
* The *[Fong Sai Yuk](/wiki/Fong_Sai-yuk_(film) "Fong Sai-yuk (film)")* films about another [Chinese folk hero](/wiki/Fong_Sai-yuk "Fong Sai-yuk").

Li starred in the 1995 film *[High Risk](/wiki/High_Risk_(1995_film) "High Risk (1995 film)")*, where Li plays a Captain who becomes disillusioned after his wife is murdered by crime lords. Along the way, he pairs up with a wacky sell-out actor, Frankie (played by [Jacky Cheung](/wiki/Jacky_Cheung "Jacky Cheung")), and proceeds to engage in a series of violent battles in a high-rise building. The setting is similar to that of *[Die Hard](/wiki/Die_Hard "Die Hard")* and both their Chinese film titles. This movie is notable in that director [Wong Jing](/wiki/Wong_Jing "Wong Jing") had such a terrible experience working with [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") in Jing's previous film *[City Hunter](/wiki/City_Hunter_(1993_film) "City Hunter (1993 film)")* that he chose to make Cheung's character a biting satire of Chan. Li would later publicly apologise to Chan for taking part in it.

Li had two [wuxia](/wiki/Wuxia "Wuxia") feature films released in 2011, *[The Sorcerer and the White Snake](/wiki/The_Sorcerer_and_the_White_Snake "The Sorcerer and the White Snake")* and *[Flying Swords of Dragon Gate](/wiki/Flying_Swords_of_Dragon_Gate "Flying Swords of Dragon Gate")*; the latter was helmed by [Tsui Hark](/wiki/Tsui_Hark "Tsui Hark").

To promote [tai chi](/wiki/Tai_chi "Tai chi"), in 2012, Li starred in a film titled *Tai Chi* and co-produced the movie with Chen Kuo-Fu. Li portrayed tai chi master [Yang Luchan](/wiki/Yang_Luchan "Yang Luchan").[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: International career")]

In 1998, he made his international film debut in *[Lethal Weapon 4](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_4 "Lethal Weapon 4")* which also marked the first time he had ever played a villain in a film. He agreed to do *Lethal Weapon 4* after the producer [Joel Silver](/wiki/Joel_Silver "Joel Silver") promised to give him the leading role in his next film, *[Romeo Must Die](/wiki/Romeo_Must_Die "Romeo Must Die")* (2000), alongside singer [Aaliyah](/wiki/Aaliyah "Aaliyah"). The film became a box office hit. Though Li spoke very little English at the time of production, his performance as Chinese mafia hitman Wah Sing Ku was praised.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Li turned down [Chow Yun-fat](/wiki/Chow_Yun-fat "Chow Yun-fat")'s role in *[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon")* (2000) because he promised his wife that he would not make any films during her pregnancy.[[41]](#cite_note-41) He also turned down the role of [Seraph](/wiki/Seraph_(The_Matrix) "Seraph (The Matrix)") in *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")* trilogy, based on his belief that the role was not one which required his skills and that the films were iconic and stunning enough without adding his name to the cast list. Li was also cast as [Kato](/wiki/Kato_(The_Green_Hornet) "Kato (The Green Hornet)") in *[The Green Hornet](/wiki/The_Green_Hornet_(2011_film) "The Green Hornet (2011 film)")* when the film was still in development in 2000. In 2001, it was moved to another studio. When the film was moved on again and released in 2011, the role of Kato was portrayed by [Jay Chou](/wiki/Jay_Chou "Jay Chou").

In 2001, he appeared in two more films: *[The One](/wiki/The_One_(2001_film) "The One (2001 film)")*, which was the first of his films with [Jason Statham](/wiki/Jason_Statham "Jason Statham"), and *[Kiss of the Dragon](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Dragon "Kiss of the Dragon")* opposite [Bridget Fonda](/wiki/Bridget_Fonda "Bridget Fonda") which did moderately well at the box office. In July 2001, Li agreed to produce and star in an action film with [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") which was to be released in 2002 or 2003, but no further news of their collaboration surfaced until 2006. In 2002, the period martial arts epic film *[Hero](/wiki/Hero_(2002_film) "Hero (2002 film)")* was released in the Chinese market. This film was both a commercial and critical success and became the highest-grossing motion picture in [Chinese film history](/wiki/Cinema_of_China "Cinema of China") at the time.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In 2003 he reunited with producer Joel Silver for the action thriller film *[Cradle 2 the Grave](/wiki/Cradle_2_the_Grave "Cradle 2 the Grave")* where he starred alongside rapper [DMX](/wiki/DMX_(rapper) "DMX (rapper)") and fellow martial artist [Mark Dacascos](/wiki/Mark_Dacascos "Mark Dacascos"). In 2004, Li lent his likeness, voice and provided motion capture work for the video game *[Jet Li: Rise to Honor](/wiki/Jet_Li:_Rise_to_Honor "Jet Li: Rise to Honor")*.

Li was presented the Visionary Award by East West Players, the oldest [Asian American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") theatre in the United States,[[43]](#cite_note-43) in 2002 by contemporary [John Woo](/wiki/John_Woo "John Woo"). The award recognizes "artists who have raised the visibility of the Asian Pacific American community through theater, film and television."[[44]](#cite_note-44) He delivered his acceptance speech in his native language of Mandarin.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Li took on a more serious role in the 2005 film, *[Unleashed](/wiki/Unleashed_(2005_film) "Unleashed (2005 film)")* (a.k.a. *Danny the Dog*), where he portrayed an adult with the mentality of a child who has been raised like an animal. Although his martial arts skills were used extensively, it was a somber film with more depth than had been previously seen in Li's films, and co-starred dramatic actors [Bob Hoskins](/wiki/Bob_Hoskins "Bob Hoskins") and [Morgan Freeman](/wiki/Morgan_Freeman "Morgan Freeman").

In 2006, the [martial arts film](/wiki/Martial_arts_film "Martial arts film") epic *[Fearless](/wiki/Fearless_(2006_film) "Fearless (2006 film)")*, was released worldwide. Although he will continue to make martial arts films, *Fearless* is his last wushu martial arts film.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) In *Fearless*, he played [Huo Yuanjia](/wiki/Huo_Yuanjia "Huo Yuanjia"), the real-life founder of [Chin Woo Athletic Association](/wiki/Chin_Woo_Athletic_Association "Chin Woo Athletic Association"), who reportedly defeated foreign boxers and Japanese martial artists in publicised events at a time when China's power was seen as eroding. Together with the film *[Fist of Legend](/wiki/Fist_of_Legend "Fist of Legend")*, Li has portrayed both Chen Jun, the student and avenger of Huo Yuanjia (a.k.a. Fok Yun Gap), as well as Huo Yuanjia himself. *Fearless* was released on 26 January 2006 in Hong Kong, followed by a 22 September 2006 release in the United States where it reached second place in its first weekend.

> I stepped into the martial arts movie market when I was only 16. I think I have proved my ability in this field and it won't make sense for me to continue for another five or 10 years. Huo Yuanjia is a conclusion to my life as a martial arts star.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Li has stated in an interview with the *Shenzhen Daily* newspaper that this will be his last martial arts epic, which is also stated in the film's television promotions. However, he plans to continue his film career in other genres. Specifically, he plans to continue acting in epic action and martial arts films dealing more with religious and philosophical issues.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Li's 2007 Hollywood film, *[War](/wiki/War_(2007_film) "War (2007 film)")*, was released in August of that year, and re-teamed him with actor [Jason Statham](/wiki/Jason_Statham "Jason Statham"), who previously starred with him in *The One*, and action choreographer [Corey Yuen](/wiki/Corey_Yuen "Corey Yuen"). *War* raked in a disappointing [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")23 million at the box office, becoming one of Li's lowest grossers in America; however, it was a hit on video, accumulating nearly US$52 million in rental revenue, more than doubling its box office take.[[51]](#cite_note-51) With the exception of *[Romeo Must Die](/wiki/Romeo_Must_Die "Romeo Must Die")* and the worldwide release of *[Hero](/wiki/Hero_(2002_film) "Hero (2002 film)")*, most of Li's American/Western films have been only modest hits like *[Kiss of the Dragon](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Dragon "Kiss of the Dragon")*, *[The One](/wiki/The_One_(2001_film) "The One (2001 film)")*, *[Unleashed](/wiki/Unleashed_(2005_film) "Unleashed (2005 film)")*, *[Cradle 2 the Grave](/wiki/Cradle_2_the_Grave "Cradle 2 the Grave")*, and the worldwide release of *[Fearless](/wiki/Fearless_(2006_film) "Fearless (2006 film)")*.

> He has a very good control of the strength of every fist and kick. In the past, he used too much strength, those that get hit would be in great pain. He's the archetype of power. […] Yes, he's still the best.

— [Yuen Woo-ping](/wiki/Yuen_Woo-ping "Yuen Woo-ping") martial arts choreographer[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Li was appointed by the Chinese Wushu Association as the "Image Ambassador of Wushu" (or [IWUF](/wiki/International_Wushu_Federation "International Wushu Federation") ambassador) at the [2007 World Wushu Championships](/wiki/2007_World_Wushu_Championships "2007 World Wushu Championships") in Beijing. In late 2007, Li returned again to China to participate in the China/Hong Kong co-production of the period war film *[The Warlords](/wiki/The_Warlords "The Warlords")* with [Andy Lau](/wiki/Andy_Lau "Andy Lau") and [Takeshi Kaneshiro](/wiki/Takeshi_Kaneshiro "Takeshi Kaneshiro"). This film, with its focus on dramatics rather than martial arts, netted Li the [Hong Kong Film Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Award_for_Best_Actor "Hong Kong Film Award for Best Actor").

Li and fellow martial arts veteran [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") finally appeared together onscreen for the first time in *[The Forbidden Kingdom](/wiki/The_Forbidden_Kingdom "The Forbidden Kingdom")*, which began filming in May 2007 and was released to critical and commercial success on 18 April 2008. The film was based on the legend of the [Monkey King](/wiki/Monkey_King "Monkey King") from the Chinese folk novel *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")*.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Li also starred as the lead villain Emperor Han in the fantasy action film *[The Mummy: Tomb of the Dragon Emperor](/wiki/The_Mummy:_Tomb_of_the_Dragon_Emperor "The Mummy: Tomb of the Dragon Emperor")* with actors [Brendan Fraser](/wiki/Brendan_Fraser "Brendan Fraser"), [Isabella Leong](/wiki/Isabella_Leong "Isabella Leong") and [Michelle Yeoh](/wiki/Michelle_Yeoh "Michelle Yeoh").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 2009, he launched his own fitness program, Wuji[[56]](#cite_note-56)—which consists of elements of martial arts, [yoga](/wiki/Yoga "Yoga") and [pilates](/wiki/Pilates "Pilates")—and [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") launched a special clothing line for it that bears the initials of "JL".[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) After a one-year hiatus from filmmaking, Li returned to acting in 2010, portraying a mercenary in the film *[The Expendables](/wiki/The_Expendables_(2010_film) "The Expendables (2010 film)")*, teaming up with action stars [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone"), [Jason Statham](/wiki/Jason_Statham "Jason Statham"), [Dolph Lundgren](/wiki/Dolph_Lundgren "Dolph Lundgren"), [Mickey Rourke](/wiki/Mickey_Rourke "Mickey Rourke"), [Eric Roberts](/wiki/Eric_Roberts "Eric Roberts"), [Steve Austin](/wiki/Stone_Cold_Steve_Austin "Stone Cold Steve Austin"), [Terry Crews](/wiki/Terry_Crews "Terry Crews"), and [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture"). It was the third time he had teamed up with Statham. In 2012, he reprised his role briefly in the sequel *[The Expendables 2](/wiki/The_Expendables_2 "The Expendables 2")* and returned for the third film *[The Expendables 3](/wiki/The_Expendables_3 "The Expendables 3")* in 2014. Li was initially stated to be appearing with [Vin Diesel](/wiki/Vin_Diesel "Vin Diesel") in *[XXX: Return of Xander Cage](/wiki/XXX:_Return_of_Xander_Cage "XXX: Return of Xander Cage")*, but according to a Facebook post by Diesel, Li was replaced by [Donnie Yen](/wiki/Donnie_Yen "Donnie Yen").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Li plays the Emperor of China for the 2020 live action movie, *[Mulan](/wiki/Mulan_(2020_film) "Mulan (2020 film)")*.[[62]](#cite_note-LiCast-62)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life")]

In 1987, Li married Huang Qiuyan,[[63]](#cite_note-63) his Beijing Wushu Team fellow member and *[Kids From Shaolin](/wiki/Kids_From_Shaolin "Kids From Shaolin")* co-star. They have two daughters, Si and Taimi.[[64]](#cite_note-64) He met actress [Nina Li Chi](/wiki/Nina_Li_Chi "Nina Li Chi") during the filming of *[Dragon Fight](/wiki/Dragon_Fight "Dragon Fight")* (1989) and divorced Huang in 1990. In 1999, Li married Nina at his villa in [Arcadia, California](/wiki/Arcadia,_California "Arcadia, California"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] which he owned from 1998 to 2004.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The couple have two daughters, Jane (born 2000) and Jada (born 2002).[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In 1997, Li obtained US citizenship, which he renounced in April 2009.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) In July 2009, Amy Zhou Weiyan, then executive chairperson of the [One Foundation](/wiki/One_Foundation "One Foundation"), confirmed that Li had become a Singaporean citizen.[[70]](#cite_note-news-70) In 2011, Li confirmed his Singaporean citizenship.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In 1998, Li converted to [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") when he was promoting *Lethal Weapon 4* in Taiwan.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-UDN_2023-73) His master is Lho Kunsang.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In 2013, Li revealed that he suffers from [hyperthyroidism](/wiki/Hyperthyroidism "Hyperthyroidism") and had been dealing with the issue for the past three years.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In 2016, Li stated that he was accepting fewer film offers due to his charity work rather than his illness, from which he had recovered.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Li likes to play badminton and table tennis, ride his bicycle, read and meditate in his free time. He collects rare [Tibetan beads](/wiki/Buddhist_prayer_beads "Buddhist prayer beads").[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

## Views on life and martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Views on life and martial arts")]

Li, as a Buddhist, believes that the difficulties of everyday life can be overcome with the help of religious philosophies.[[79]](#cite_note-strong-in-life-79) He thinks that fame is not something he can control; therefore, he does not care about it.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

> I never say to myself I'm the best fighter in the world. If someone learns martial arts solely to pick fights on the street, to lean on it as a keystone weapon in conflicts, to use it to bully and intimidate others – then that person, in my opinion, cannot be considered a true martial artist.[[81]](#cite_note-fight-81)

According to Li, everything he has ever wanted to tell the world can be found in three of his films: the message of *[Hero](/wiki/Hero_(2002_film) "Hero (2002 film)")* is that the suffering of one person can never be as significant as the suffering of a nation; *[Unleashed](/wiki/Unleashed_(2005_film) "Unleashed (2005 film)")* shows that violence is never a solution; and *[Fearless](/wiki/Fearless_(2006_film) "Fearless (2006 film)")* tells that the biggest enemy of a person is himself. Li thinks that the greatest weapon is a smile and the largest power is love.[[82]](#cite_note-time-82)

About Wushu, he said that he believes the essence of martial arts is not power or speed but inner harmony, and considers it a sad development that today's Wushu championships place greater emphasis on form than on the essence of being a martial artist. He believes Wushu now lacks individuality and competitors move like machines, whereas according to his views Wushu should not be considered a race where the fastest athlete wins. He would like to see Wushu as a form of art, where artists have a distinctive style. Li blames the new competition rules that, according to him, place limitations on martial artists.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Li believes that Wushu is not primarily for self-defense and instead of trying to play the hero people should think about peaceful resolutions of conflicts and call the police if necessary:

> "A gun outdoes years of martial arts training in a split second. Like I've said many times before, it is important to differentiate between movies and reality. The hero in movies may be able to knock the gun off his opponent and save the day, but in real life – probably that is not the case."[[84]](#cite_note-84)

He has also stated that he has never had to use his martial arts skills in a real-life fight and he does not wish to, either.[[81]](#cite_note-fight-81)

### Taiji Zen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Taiji Zen")]

In 2011, Li founded Taiji Zen, along with co-founder Executive chairman [Jack Ma](/wiki/Jack_Ma "Jack Ma"). Taiji Zen combined the martial art of [tai chi](/wiki/Tai_chi "Tai chi") (*taijiquan* in Chinese) with practices such as [meditation](/wiki/Meditation "Meditation"). It packaged these into several different classes and online programs.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Jet_Li_-_Annual_Meeting_of_the_New_Champions_Tianjin_2008.jpg/250px-Jet_Li_-_Annual_Meeting_of_the_New_Champions_Tianjin_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jet_Li_-_Annual_Meeting_of_the_New_Champions_Tianjin_2008.jpg)

Li speaks at the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") in Tianjin, China, September 2008

Li has been a "philanthropic ambassador" of the [Red Cross Society of China](/wiki/Red_Cross_Society_of_China "Red Cross Society of China") since January 2006. He contributed 500,000 yuan (US$62,500) of box office revenues from his film *Fearless* to the Red Cross' psychological sunshine project, which promotes mental health.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In April 2007, touched by his life-shaking experience in the Maldives when he was close to dying during the [2004 tsunami](/wiki/Effect_of_the_2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_on_the_Maldives "Effect of the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake on the Maldives"), Li formed his own non-profit foundation called [One Foundation](/wiki/One_Foundation "One Foundation").[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) The One Foundation supports international disaster relief efforts in conjunction with the Red Cross as well as other efforts, including mental health awareness and suicide prevention. Since the starting of the foundation, Li has been involved with recovery efforts in seven disasters, including the [2008 Sichuan earthquake](/wiki/2008_Sichuan_earthquake "2008 Sichuan earthquake")[[89]](#cite_note-newsweek.com-89) and [Typhoon Morakot](/wiki/Typhoon_Morakot "Typhoon Morakot") in Taiwan.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In the [2013 Lushan earthquake](/wiki/2013_Lushan_earthquake "2013 Lushan earthquake") in [Ya'an](/wiki/Ya%27an "Ya'an"), Sichuan, Li and other members of the entertainment sector were the first to appeal for donations of money, goods and materials to help the victims of the disaster.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) [Wu Jing](/wiki/Wu_Jing_(actor) "Wu Jing (actor)") was a One Foundation volunteer and helped in the effort.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Li discussed his commitment to philanthropy in an interview with the December 2009 issue of *Alliance* magazine, stating that "grassroots non-government organizations can help the government in its blind spots. Government relief is not always detail-oriented. Grassroots NGOs can't be as big as a government effort, but they need to be flexible and independent."[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In September 2010, he was appointed by the International Red Cross as the first Good Will Ambassador. He posted online, saying:

> "Today I signed a deal with the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies – IFRC – to become the FIRST goodwill ambassador in the history of this humanitarian organization. I am very honored! At the same time, I will not pause to celebrate, but instead keep pushing forward and do my best to help the world! Thank you all once again for your support and belief in me!"[[95]](#cite_note-95)

It was also announced in September 2010, when Li was attending his wax unveiling ceremony in Hong Kong Madame Tussauds, that Li would be meeting Bill Gates and Warren Buffett to talk about charity work.[[96]](#cite_note-96) "Three days ago, I received an email from Gates, hoping I could make time because he and Buffett hoped I could go for a 30-minute chat before the dinner about the future we face as human beings, so I will go," Li said.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jet_Li&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Filmography")]

Main article: [Jet Li filmography](/wiki/Jet_Li_filmography "Jet Li filmography")

By US [box office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") statistics, the most successful Jet Li film as of August 2010 is *[Lethal Weapon 4](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_4 "Lethal Weapon 4")*, which grossed over $130 million domestically, while the second is *[The Expendables](/wiki/The_Expendables_(2010_film) "The Expendables (2010 film)")* with over $103 million.[[98]](#cite_note-98) *[Hero](/wiki/Hero_(2002_film) "Hero (2002 film)")* is the third most successful foreign language film in the US,[[99]](#cite_note-boxforeign-99) and one of the most critically acclaimed Li movies.[[100]](#cite_note-jet-rotten-100) *[Fearless](/wiki/Fearless_(2006_film) "Fearless (2006 film)")* is the seventh most successful foreign language film of all time in the US.[[99]](#cite_note-boxforeign-99) From an aggregated critical point of view, the best acclaimed Li movie is *[Fist of Legend](/wiki/Fist_of_Legend "Fist of Legend")* (Rotten Tomatoes: 100%) and the worst is *[The One](/wiki/The_One_(2001_film) "The One (2001 film)")* (Rotten Tomatoes: 13%).[[100]](#cite_note-jet-rotten-100)
